verses.text: Learning of the decree, Mordecai tears his clothes, puts on sackcloth, and cries bitterly, and mourning spreads among the Jews everywhere. Grief drives them to lament and, in it, to seek God. The first cry of the afflicted is heard in heaven even when His name is unspoken.

Learning of the decree, Mordecai tears his clothes, puts on sackcloth, and cries bitterly, and mourning spreads among the Jews everywhere. Grief drives them to lament and, in it, to seek God. The first cry of the afflicted is heard in heaven even when His name is unspoken.

Learning of the decree, Mordecai tears his clothes, puts on sackcloth, and cries bitterly, and mourning spreads among the Jews everywhere. Grief drives them to lament and, in it, to seek God. The first cry of the afflicted is heard in heaven even when His name is unspoken.

Esther, troubled, sends garments to Mordecai, but he refuses comfort, and she sends Hathach to learn the cause. She must be drawn from palace ease to her people's peril. God uses our distress to wake us to the needs around us.

Esther, troubled, sends garments to Mordecai, but he refuses comfort, and she sends Hathach to learn the cause. She must be drawn from palace ease to her people's peril. God uses our distress to wake us to the needs around us.

Esther, troubled, sends garments to Mordecai, but he refuses comfort, and she sends Hathach to learn the cause. She must be drawn from palace ease to her people's peril. God uses our distress to wake us to the needs around us.

Mordecai sends Esther the full account and a copy of the decree, charging her to go plead with the king for her people. The crisis is laid plainly before her. There are moments when knowledge becomes a summons we cannot refuse.

Mordecai sends Esther the full account and a copy of the decree, charging her to go plead with the king for her people. The crisis is laid plainly before her. There are moments when knowledge becomes a summons we cannot refuse.

Mordecai sends Esther the full account and a copy of the decree, charging her to go plead with the king for her people. The crisis is laid plainly before her. There are moments when knowledge becomes a summons we cannot refuse.

Esther answers that none may approach the king unsummoned on pain of death, and she has not been called for thirty days. Fear and danger hold her back. Even the well-placed shrink when duty means risking everything.

Esther answers that none may approach the king unsummoned on pain of death, and she has not been called for thirty days. Fear and danger hold her back. Even the well-placed shrink when duty means risking everything.

Esther answers that none may approach the king unsummoned on pain of death, and she has not been called for thirty days. Fear and danger hold her back. Even the well-placed shrink when duty means risking everything.

Mordecai replies that she will not escape by silence; deliverance will come another way, but she will perish — 'who knoweth whether thou art come to the kingdom for such a time as this?' God's purposes will stand, with us or without us. The honour is to be His willing instrument in the appointed hour.

Mordecai replies that she will not escape by silence; deliverance will come another way, but she will perish — 'who knoweth whether thou art come to the kingdom for such a time as this?' God's purposes will stand, with us or without us. The honour is to be His willing instrument in the appointed hour.

Esther asks all to fast three days, resolving, 'I will go in unto the king... and if I perish, I perish.' Faith counts the cost and obeys. When duty is clear, the believer commits the outcome to God and steps forward, come what may.
